title: Assigning a Persona
concept: 'You are an expert X' framing and how it shifts tone, vocabulary, and depth of response.

exercises.prompt: Before we dive in: when you ask an AI to 'pretend to be' someone, what do you think changes in its answer?

options.text: The way it talks and explains things
The AI gains secret new knowledge

exercises.explain: Great instinct! Assigning a persona mainly changes HOW the AI communicates — its tone, word choice, and depth — not what secret facts it has access to. We'll dig into exactly how this works in this lesson.

cards.title: Same Question, Totally Different Answer?
cards.body: What if you could ask an AI the exact same question twice and get a totally different-sounding answer each time — just by telling it who to pretend to be first? That's the power of persona prompting.

cards.title: The 'You Are an Expert X' Framing
cards.body: This is a prompting trick where you start by telling the AI to act as a specific expert, like 'You are an expert chef' or 'You are an expert lawyer.' It doesn't give the AI new facts, but it shifts HOW the AI answers: word choice, tone, and how deep or technical the explanation gets.

cards.title: Like Hiring a Costume
cards.body: Asking an AI to imagine it's a persona is like putting on a costume before a play. The actor is the same person underneath, but the costume changes how they walk, talk, and act on stage. A persona prompt is a costume for the AI's words.

cards.title: Same Question, Two Personas
cards.body: Ask 'How do I boil an egg?' with 'You are a 5-star chef' and you get precise timing, terms like 'simmer' and 'shock in ice water.' Ask the same question with 'You are a tired college student' and you get 'just dunk it in hot water for like 10 mins, done.'

cards.title: Myth: Persona Prompts Add New Knowledge
cards.body: Saying 'You are an expert doctor' doesn't hand the AI secret medical knowledge it didn't already have. It reshapes tone, vocabulary, and structure of the response using knowledge the model already learned during training.

cards.title: No Persona vs. Expert Persona
cards.body: Compare a plain prompt to one with an assigned expert persona and notice how the response's vocabulary and depth shift.

left.text: 'Explain gravity.' → Simple, generic, medium-depth answer with everyday words.

right.text: 'Explain gravity.' → Deeper answer using terms like 'mass,' 'curvature,' and more precise structure.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the clearest example of 'You are an expert X' persona framing?

options.text: You are an expert geologist. Explain how volcanoes form to a curious 8-year-old.
Volcanoes are made of lava and rock.

exercises.explain: Option B explicitly assigns an expert persona ('expert geologist') AND gives context about the audience. That combo is exactly what persona/role prompting looks like — the other options are plain questions or statements with no role assigned.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using 'You are an expert X' persona framing guarantees the AI's facts will be more accurate.
exercises.explain: False. Persona framing shifts style — tone, vocabulary, depth — but it doesn't magically add accuracy or new verified facts. The AI still relies on what it already learned; the costume changes the performance, not the actor's underlying knowledge.

exercises.sentence: Telling an AI 'You are an expert historian' mainly changes the tone and vocabulary of the response, not the underlying facts the model already knows.
exercises.explain: Persona prompting reshapes HOW something is said — the tone, vocabulary, and depth — rather than injecting brand new facts the AI didn't already know.

exercises.prompt: Two students used the SAME persona prompt 'You are an expert nutritionist' to ask about eating vegetables. One answer is AI-generated from that exact prompt, and one is a random unprompted answer. Spot the one that best matches the persona.

options.text: Eat veggies, they're good for you I guess. Broccoli is green.
Incorporating cruciferous vegetables like broccoli supports micronutrient intake, particularly vitamin K and folate, which are essential for metabolic health.
idk vegetables are fine sometimes eat some

exercises.explain: Option B uses precise, technical vocabulary ('cruciferous,' 'micronutrient,' 'metabolic health') that reflects an expert nutritionist persona. The other two are vague and casual, showing no sign of an assigned expert role shaping the response.

exercises.prompt: You're building a homework-help chatbot for 10-year-olds explaining fractions. Which persona instruction fits BEST?

options.text: You are a strict college math professor grading a thesis defense.
You are a friendly elementary school teacher who explains ideas with fun, simple examples.
You are a Nobel Prize-winning physicist writing a technical research paper.
You are a robot that only outputs numbers with no explanation.

exercises.explain: Persona prompting works best when the persona matches your AUDIENCE and GOAL, not just the topic. Since the audience is 10-year-olds, you want a persona built for simple, friendly, patient explanations — not a professor or physicist persona, which would push the tone toward jargon and density that kids can't follow. Matching persona to audience is the real skill behind 'You are an expert X' framing. That's a wrap on Unit 411 — you've leveled up your prompting toolkit!
